Investor Anatomy
US Credit Conditions
Home
US Credit Conditions
Integrates credit spreads, systemic-stress indicators, and private-credit growth signals to assess the prevailing US credit-conditions regime.

Regime Assessment:

  • Regime: Transitional / Fragile Balance
  • Regime Confidence Index: Low Confidence
  • Stability: Fragile

Why This Regime:

  • High-weight private credit acceleration (4) suggests an expansionary environment.
  • This is directly countered by a high-weight bearish systemic stress signal (5).
  • The transitional label reflects the conflict between cyclical credit velocity and tactical risk-off sentiment.
  • Credit conditions were downweighted due to mixed signals and internal conflict between volatility and spreads (3).

Alignment & Tensions:

  • Corporate spreads and aggregate stress levels both remain within neutral historical ranges (1)(2).
  • Acute tension exists between accelerating bank credit (4) and elevated financial stress Z-scores (5).
  • Tensions do not overturn the regime because neither driver has yet forced corporate spreads out of neutral (1).

Scenario Balance:

  • Dominant scenario: A fragile neutral state where credit availability offsets rising liquidity concerns.
  • Primary upside risk: A return to expansionary easing triggered by volatility compression and sustained loan demand.
  • Primary downside risk: A shift to risk-negative conditions if systemic stress causes corporate spreads to widen.

What Would Change the Regime:

  • Private credit impulse falling below 0.75 for two consecutive months (4).
  • A 3-month high-yield spread increase exceeding 50 basis points (1).
  • The financial stress robust Z-score falling and sustaining below the 0.5 threshold (5).
Inputs
Credit Spreads Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement: Given a HY_OAS of 3.21 and a CSC_z score of 0.03, this setup aligns with Range-biased price paths and Normal volatility, where the dominant risk is Mean reversion, not Regime shift. (1)
  • The signal is currently in a NORMAL regime, reflecting credit conditions aligned with historical averages. (1)
  • Conviction Band: Medium; Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Deteriorating; Threshold Proximity: Moderate; Revision Sensitivity: Moderate.

Methodology Applied:

  • The NORMAL regime is defined by a HY_OAS between 3.5% and 5% with a CSC_z between -0.5 and 0.5. (1)
  • TIGHTENING is triggered if HY spreads rise > 50bps in 3 months or the CSC_z exceeds 0.5. (1)
  • Rising signal values imply a risk-off transition, tightening financial conditions, and economic slowdown. (1)
  • Dataset: ICE BofA US High Yield and Corporate Master OAS (Latest: 2026-03-31). (1)

Key Dynamics:

  • The primary driver is the recent widening in HY_OAS (3.21) and IG_OAS (0.88), pushing the CSC_z into positive territory. (1)
  • Positive momentum is observed, with HY_OAS rising 33bps over the last three months (2.88 to 3.21). (1)
  • Conditional Invalidation: A 3-month HY spread increase exceeding 50bps would trigger a TIGHTENING regime. (1)
  • Revision window cannot be assessed from provided content.

Scenario Balance:

  • Base case dominant: Persistence of the NORMAL regime as spreads stabilise near neutral levels.
  • Upside risk: A reversal in momentum leads to re-entry into the EASY regime (HY < 3.5%).
  • Downside risk: Accelerated widening triggers a TIGHTENING regime and defensive positioning.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months), reflecting typical credit cycle lead properties. (1)
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Medium, as credit spreads serve as a core gauge of funding stress. (1)

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs US Credit Conditions by measuring the cost of corporate borrowing relative to risk-free rates. (1)
  • Economic mechanism: Directional increases reflect rising default expectations and higher liquidity/risk premia. (1)
  • Cycle position: Not determined.

Regime Context:

  • The NORMAL regime has been persistent for approximately 8 months, following a brief EASY excursion. (1)
  • Direction of change: Strengthening toward the upper bounds of neutral as spreads widen from recent lows. (1)

Model Limitations:

  • 1-2 business day reporting lag and potential for bid-ask spread widening to artificially inflate levels. (1)

Data & References:

  • Latest Observation: 2026-03-31; HY_OAS (3.21), IG_OAS (0.88), CSC_z (0.03). (1)
  • Additional public datasets: VIX index for volatility confirmation and Federal Reserve liquidity measures. (1)
Financial Stress Composite Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement (mandatory): Given a composite Fin_Stress value of -0.0577, supported by a rising Z_STL of 0.497 and a subdued Z_HY of -0.116, this setup aligns with Range-biased price paths and Normal volatility, where the dominant risk is Mean reversion, not Regime shift.
  • The signal currently occupies the Neutral regime, reflecting a steady increase in aggregate stress levels toward the historical mean. (1)
  • Conviction Band: Medium; Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Deteriorating; Threshold Proximity: Moderate; Revision Sensitivity: Unknown.

Methodology Applied:

  • Composite values between -0.75 and 0.75 indicate a Neutral regime characterized by market stability and historical norms (Fin_Stress). (1)
  • Sub-component analysis distinguishes between credit-led stress (HY_OAS) and policy-led stress (T10Y2Y). (1)
  • Rising composite values signify a directional transition toward risk-off and economic slowdown (Fin_Stress_Regime). (1)
  • Financial Stress Composite Signal: latest observation 2026-03-31.

Key Dynamics:

  • The primary driver is the increase in the St. Louis Fed Stress component (Z_STL), which rose from -0.68 to 0.49 over the lookback period. (1)
  • Stabilization is evident in high-yield spreads (Z_HY), which remain slightly below mean levels, preventing a move into the high-stress regime. (1)
  • The yield curve inversion (Z_INV) has moderated, providing a counter-cyclical offset to rising funding liquidity concerns. (1)
  • Conditional Invalidation: A move in the composite index above 0.75 would signal a shift to High Stress. (1)
  • Persistence Assessment: The signal has remained in Neutral for over 24 months, though momentum is currently weakening. (1)

Scenario Balance:

  • Base case dominant: Continued Neutral stability as credit spreads offset funding liquidity tightening.
  • Upside risk: Return to Low Stress triggered by a renewed collapse in market volatility (VIX) and credit premia.
  • Downside risk: Transition to High Stress triggered by a synchronous spike in STLFSI4 and corporate default risk.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Tactical (weeks): The signal identifies high-frequency shifts in funding conditions and risk appetite. (1)
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Medium; neutral readings provide a baseline but lack the urgency of extreme regime shifts.

Macro Relevance:

  • This signal informs the Systemic Stress and Funding Liquidity macro dimensions. (1)
  • The economic mechanism implied is the intersection of volatility, credit risk, and policy-driven term structure. (1)
  • Cycle position: Not determined by this methodology.
  • Interacts with yield curve and credit signals to confirm if policy adjustments are impacting real-economy funding. (1)

Regime Context:

  • Persistent Neutral regime; the signal is currently transitioning from an accommodative bias toward the neutral center-line. (1)
  • Direction of change: Weakening (reduction in accommodation, increasing aggregate stress). (1)

Model Limitations:

  • Monthly frequency may fail to capture intra-month liquidity spikes or crises. (1)
  • Potential double-counting exists between the St. Louis Fed index and High Yield option-adjusted spreads. (1)

Data & References:

  • Financial Stress Composite Signal (Latest: 2026-03-31). (1)
  • The most influential datapoints are the STLFSI4 z-score (0.497) and the T10Y2Y spread (0.56). (1)
  • Monitoring the TED Spread and VIX futures would provide additional depth to funding stress assessments.
Credit Conditions Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement (mandatory): Given a neutral Credit_Conditions index of 0.31, driven by a high VIX z-score of 2.28 and stable corporate spreads, this setup aligns with Range-biased price paths and Elevated volatility, where the dominant risk is Regime shift, not Mean reversion.
  • The signal remains in a Neutral regime, but is trending toward the tightening threshold due to equity market uncertainty (1).
  • Conviction Band: Low; Interpretation Confidence: Mixed Signals; Internal Conflict Flag: Yes. Signal Stability Assessment: Volatile; Threshold Proximity: Near; Revision Sensitivity: Unknown.

Methodology Applied:

  • Index values between -0.75 and 0.75 indicate a neutral funding environment and range-bound systemic stress (1).
  • Component decomposition is required to distinguish between funding-driven stress (spreads) and sentiment-driven stress (VIX) (1).
  • Rising values indicate a transition toward risk-off conditions and credit contraction (1).
  • Credit Conditions Signal: Latest observation 2026-03-31 (1).

Key Dynamics:

  • The primary driver is the VIX (27.44), which has spiked to a z-score of 2.28, signaling acute sentiment-driven stress (1).
  • Corporate spreads (HY_OAS at 3.21; BBB_OAS at 1.11) remain near historical norms, offsetting the volatility component.
  • Internal Tension: Significant divergence between stable corporate funding costs and high equity market fear.
  • Conditional Invalidation: An index move above 0.75 would trigger a "Tightening" regime and a mandatory risk-off posture (1).
  • Signal stability is deteriorating as the composite rises sharply from -0.34 to 0.31 in three months.

Scenario Balance:

  • Base Case dominant: Funding remains available despite rising equity volatility; spreads remain anchored.
  • Most plausible upside risk: Volatility compression leads to a return to the "Easing" regime.
  • Most plausible downside risk: High volatility spills into credit markets, widening spreads and forcing a "Tightening" regime.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Tactical (weeks) due to the heavy weighting of the volatile VIX component.
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: Medium; while the regime is neutral, the proximity to a tightening threshold warrants close monitoring.

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs the dimension of systemic liquidity and risk premia within the credit transmission mechanism (1).
  • Implied economic mechanism: Rising VIX without spread widening suggests a "sentiment shock" rather than a fundamental "liquidity crunch."
  • Cycle position: Not determined.
  • Interacts with VIX and Credit Spreads signals to confirm if stress is broadening across asset classes (1).

Regime Context:

  • Persistent Neutral regime (13 months duration) following an exit from "Easing" in early 2025.
  • Direction of change: Strengthening toward the tightening boundary (0.75).

Model Limitations:

  • EMA(3) smoothing introduces lag in identifying rapid stress inflections (1).
  • VIX includes non-credit equity variance risk, potentially overstating funding stress.

Data & References:

  • Latest Data: March 31, 2026. Data timeliness cannot be assessed.
  • Dominant Datapoint: VIXCLS (27.44) provides the only significant positive z-score in the composite (1).
  • Secondary datasets: FRED St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index (STLFSI4) or M2 Money Supply would provide broader liquidity context.
Private Credit Impulse Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement: Given a Private_Credit_Impulse of 0.84 and a Z_TOTBKCR_ann6 of 0.84, this setup aligns with Upward-biased price paths and Normal volatility, where the dominant risk is Trend continuation, not Mean reversion (1).
  • The signal has entered the "Accelerating" regime, confirmed by two consecutive monthly prints above the 0.75 expansionary threshold (1).
  • Conviction Band: Medium. Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence. Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Improving. Threshold Proximity: Near. Revision Sensitivity: High.

Methodology Applied:

  • Regime classification: A composite score > 0.75 confirmed for two periods indicates accelerating credit growth and easing financing conditions (1).
  • Momentum Rule: Directional shifts in bank credit (TOTBKCR) and business loans (BUSLOANS) guide regime bias (1).
  • Confirmation Rule: A two-period confirmation is required to minimize noise from weekly data revisions (1).
  • Private Credit Impulse Signal (Latest observation: 2026-03-31).

Key Dynamics:

  • The primary driver is the expansion of commercial bank credit (TOTBKCR), with a six-month annualized Z-score of 0.84 (1).
  • The impulse transitioned from "Stable" to "Accelerating" in February 2026 and sustained the level through March (1).
  • Partial data for the latest period shows a dip in the composite from 1.01 to 0.84, though it remains above the critical threshold (1).
  • Conditional Invalidation: Two consecutive monthly composite prints falling below 0.75 (1).
  • Stability is currently improving as the signal moves deeper into the expansionary zone.

Scenario Balance:

  • Base case dominant: Sustained credit expansion supported by easing financing conditions.
  • Upside risk secondary: Rapid re-acceleration of business loan demand (BUSLOANS) alongside bank credit.
  • Downside risk residual: Negative data revisions to the H.8 bank series invalidating the threshold breach.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Cyclical (months) as the signal identifies mid-term shifts in liquidity and demand (1).
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: High; credit velocity is a potent leading indicator for broader economic momentum.

Macro Relevance:

  • Informs US liquidity and monetary conditions by quantifying the velocity of private-sector credit creation (1).
  • Implied economic mechanism: Easing financing conditions facilitating rising investment and consumption demand (1).
  • Cycle position: Early/mid-cycle expansion (1).
  • Typically interacts with lending standards and business sentiment to confirm pro-cyclical trends.

Regime Context:

  • The current "Accelerating" regime was newly entered following a prolonged "Stable" phase throughout late 2025 (1).
  • Direction of change: Strengthening, characterized by the break above the 0.75 z-score level (1).

Model Limitations:

  • Frequent weekly revisions to underlying commercial bank data can create signal whipsaws (1).
  • High inflation may distort nominal credit growth figures, necessitating macro-adjusted interpretation (1).

Data & References:

  • Private Credit Impulse Signal (2026-03-31) (1).
  • Datapoints: Private_Credit_Impulse (0.84), TOTBKCR (19,345), Z_TOTBKCR_ann6 (0.84) (1).
  • Data freshness: Unknown. Data timeliness cannot be assessed.
  • Lending standards (SLOOS) and nominal GDP growth would provide additional confirmatory depth.
Financial Stress Index Signal

Signal Summary:

  • Configuration statement: Given a Stress_z of 0.9499 and a STLFSI4 of -0.3677, this setup aligns with Downward-biased price paths and Elevated volatility, where the dominant risk is Regime shift, not Mean reversion.
  • The signal remains in a persistent Bearish regime, reflecting elevated systemic stress and tightening financial conditions (1).
  • Conviction Band: Medium; Interpretation Confidence: High Confidence; Internal Conflict Flag: No. Signal Stability Assessment: Deteriorating; Threshold Proximity: Far; Revision Sensitivity: Unknown.

Methodology Applied:

  • A Robust Z-score > 0.5 classifies the environment as Bearish/Risk-off, indicating tightening conditions (1).
  • Positive or rising Z-scores imply a transition to contractionary or risk-off regimes (1).
  • Financial Stress Index Signal (latest observation: 2026-03-20) (1).

Key Dynamics:

  • The primary driver is the Stress_z score of 0.9499, which significantly exceeds the 0.5 Bearish threshold (1).
  • Recent momentum shows a sharp move from Neutral in early February to a sustained Bearish state by March (1).
  • Internal tensions are absent as both the raw index and Z-score support a risk-off interpretation (1).
  • Conditional Invalidation: The Robust Z-score falling and sustaining below the 0.5 threshold (1).
  • The signal demonstrates high persistence, maintaining Bearish status for four consecutive weeks (1).

Scenario Balance:

  • Base Case dominant: Risk-off conditions persist as systemic stress remains elevated.
  • Upside risk: A rapid reversion to Neutral triggered by a compression in equity volatility or credit spreads.
  • Downside risk: Escalation to "Stressed" or "Crisis" regimes if Z-scores surge past 2.0.

Time Horizon & Aggregation:

  • Time Horizon: Tactical (weeks) based on the weekly sensitivity and reporting frequency of the STLFSI4 index (1).
  • Aggregation Weight Hint: High; systemic stress serves as a critical filter for all cyclical risk assets (1).

Macro Relevance:

  • This signal informs the "Systemic Stress" dimension, specifically tracking market risk aversion and funding liquidity (1).
  • The economic mechanism implied is tightening financial system depth, which creates a headwind for growth and risk-seeking capital flows (1).
  • Cycle position: Not determined by this methodology.
  • Typically acts as a confirming filter for credit and liquidity signals (1).

Regime Context:

  • The current Bearish regime is persistent, having been entered in late February 2026 (1).
  • Direction of change: Strengthening (into the Bearish zone), now stabilising at elevated stress levels (1).

Model Limitations:

  • Weekly frequency may lag intra-week volatility spikes; sensitive to threshold whipsaws near zero (1).

Data & References:

  • Financial Stress Index (STLFSI4), latest date 2026-03-20 (1).
  • Influential datapoints: Stress_z (0.9499) and the 4-week Bearish streak (1).
  • Additional datasets: High Yield Option-Adjusted Spreads and VIX levels would improve granularity.
Investor Anatomy - US Credit Conditions - Not Investment Advice
The information presented is for general informational purposes only and does not constitute financial or investment advice. It has been prepared without regard to individual objectives, financial situation, or needs. You should consider whether it is appropriate for your circumstances and seek independent advice where necessary.
